_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with an upward slope indicating higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting normal economic conditions. An inverted, or downward sloping curve, has historically preceded US recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.58% for June 2026, combined with a positive 10Y-2Y spread of 0.42 points, indicates the curve has returned to an upward slope after a prolonged inversion from 2022 to 2024. This contrasts with the deepest inversion point of 0.0 points in the 2s10s spread during the prior period.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread measures the difference between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields and serves as a leading recession indicator. Its inversion from 2022 to 2024 signaled elevated recession risk, but the recent re-steepening to 0.42 points suggests a normalization of market expectations. The New York Fed assigns a 31% recession probability, reflecting moderate concern without definitive signals. Importantly, the yield curve often reverts to an upward slope shortly before recessions occur, tempering the immediacy of inversion-based warnings.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's upward slope and positive 2s10s spread, alongside a 31% recession probability, suggest that recession risks persist but are not elevated at critical levels. The un-inversion of the curve implies reduced near-term recession threat compared to prior years, although vigilance remains warranted given historical patterns.
